title: Criticality around the Spinodal Point of First-Order Quantum Phase Transitions
authors: Fan Zhang, Chiao Wang, H. T. Quan
abstract: Universality and scaling are hallmarks of second-order phase transitions but are generally unexpected in first-order quantum phase transitions (FOQPTs). We present a microscopic theory showing that quantum criticality can emerge around the quantum spinodal point of FOQPTs where metastability disappears. We demonstrate that, at this instability, resonant local excitations dynamically decouple a Hilbert subspace characterized by an emergent discrete translational symmetry. Projecting the original Hamiltonian onto this subspace yields an effective Hamiltonian that exhibits a genuine second-order quantum phase transition (SOQPT) and the Kibble-Zurek scaling. We validate this framework in the tilted Ising chain which breaks Z_2 symmetry, and predict the absence of criticality in the staggered-field PXP model. This work indicates that the FOQPT dynamics is usually governed by an emergent critical point around the quantum spinodal point. Our study establishes a bridge between the dynamics of the FOQPT and SOQPT, and thus sheds new light on the long-standing conundrum of the dynamics of the FOQPT.
summary: This paper demonstrates that second-order quantum criticality can emerge at the spinodal point of first-order quantum phase transitions due to emergent symmetries in a decoupled Hilbert subspace. By mapping FOQPT dynamics to effective SOQPT models, the authors bridge the gap between different types of phase transition dynamics. This discovery explains the presence or absence of universal scaling in various quantum many-body systems.
main_problem: Understanding how universality and scaling emerge in the non-equilibrium dynamics of first-order quantum phase transitions (FOQPTs), specifically addressing why some FOQPTs exhibit criticality while others do not.
main_result: The authors show that quantum criticality can emerge around the quantum spinodal point of FOQPTs where metastability disappears, driven by an effective Hamiltonian with emergent discrete translational symmetry.
method: The study employs a microscopic theory using Schrieffer-Wolff transformations to derive effective Hamiltonians, alongside numerical techniques like TDVP, DMRG, and the Kibble-Zurek mechanism (KZM) for scaling analysis.
model_or_system: The framework is validated using the 1D quantum tilted Ising model and the PXP model with a staggered field, as well as a 1D next-nearest-neighbor (NNN) tilted Ising model.
key_observables: Density of topological defects (kink density), correlation length, longitudinal and staggered magnetization, and Z3 order parameter.
important_parameters: Quench velocity (v), longitudinal and transverse fields (hz, hx), interaction range (rho), and the spinodal point (lambda_sp).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the driving timescale significantly exceeds the intrinsic microscopic evolution timescale and treats the narrow BKT floating phase in the NNN model as a single effective second-order transition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the energy landscape and formation of constrained Hilbert space; Figure 3 shows the energy crossover and magnetization jumps in the PXP model; Figure 5 demonstrates scaling collapse of magnetization and correlation length in the NNN Ising model.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of FOQPT dynamics, presents a microscopic theory for emergent criticality at the spinodal point, validates this via the tilted Ising model, compares it to the non-critical staggered PXP model, and extends the analysis to NNN models with emergent Z3 symmetry.
why_interesting: It provides a universal mechanism for the emergence of second-order criticality from first-order transitions, which is highly relevant for understanding the dynamics of Rydberg atom arrays and other programmable quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Emergent symmetry at a quantum spinodal point. (a) Schematic energy landscape near the quantum spinodal point. Taking the all-down state as the metastable state, the spinodal point is reached when a local excitation costs zero energy, giving rise to a dense set of degenerate energy levels. (b) The degenerate states are formed by local excitations of size R on top of the metastable background. Distinct excitations must be separated by at least ρ + 1 where ρ is the interaction range of the Hamiltonian. These states define a kinetically constrained Hilbert space. (c) Within this constrained Hilbert space, the effective Hamiltonian near the quantum spinodal point exhibits an emergent...

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the tilted Ising model for different ramping rates (encoded by the color scale). Here, h∗ z = (hz −hsp z )/hx. Panels (a) and (b) show the magnetization Sz of the tilted Ising model in the unscaled and scaled variables, respectively. Panels (c) and (d) display the unscaled and scaled correlation length ξ. Panels (e) and (f) display the unscaled and scaled topological defects. Because Sz is not an order parameter of the effective Hamiltonian, it does not obey a strict scaling ansatz, and only exhibits peaks at the same positions. In contrast, the correlation length and the topological defects collapse in a better way, as shown in panels (c)-(f). The inset in panel...

FIG. 3. Magnetization at odd sites for PXP model with a staggered field. (a) Evolution for the original Hamiltonian, calculated by TDVP method. We consider a system with 259 sites, starting from δs = −0.5 at its metastable state. Another parameter is λ = 0.1. (b) Scaled evolution. We scale the vertical axis as Sodd/[2 exp(−πλ2/v) −1] and find the final value of all curves asymptotically approach 1, consistent with the Landau-Zener theory. We scale the horizontal axis as v0.5δs.
